learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate reflective practice, professional competence, interpersonal skills and expertise in the Australian community pharmacy setting; including the development of learning plans for experiential placements
Relate your knowledge, skills, and professional attributes acquired throughout the Pharmacy degree in the context of your placement experience

Reflect on your placement experience and relate it to your personal and professional development, including in the context of the National Competency Standards Framework for Pharmacists in Australia 2016, life-long learning, and future employability
Demonstrate responsiveness to physically, socially, and culturally diverse patient populations in community pharmacy contexts

assessments.due_date:
SEAL Submission 1: 2 x NCS Schema 7/08/2026 5:00 pm

SEAL Submission 2: 2 x NCS Schema 21/08/2026 5:00 pm

SEAL Submission 3: 1 x LLL Schema + 1 x Employability Schema 4/09/2026 5:00 pm
assessments.task_description:
Students will submit a total of SIX reflections:

  • SEAL Submission 1: 2 x 500 words for EACH NCS Schema (total ~1000 words)
  • SEAL Submission 2: 2 x 500 words for EACH NCS Schema (total ~1000 words)
  • SEAL Submission 3: 1 x LLL Schema (500 words) + 1 x Employability Schema (500 words) (total ~1000 words)

These reflections are a significant part of this course, and students are expected to provide a high standard of reflection for each submission. The key component of reflection is ensuring the creation of a specific plan to aid learning within the scope of the situation, and describing how this action is being undertaken.

Each reflection should not exceed 500 words. More information and marking rubrics for the SEAL reflections can be found on Learn.UQ (Blackboard) on the Assessment page. 

Students should consider their individual experiences during their Placement in order to reflect effectively within each of the different schemas described below: 

1. National Competency Standards (NCS) schema: Please read the relevant National Competency Standards (NCS) Framework for Pharmacists in Australia 2016 (available through the course Blackboard site) and familiarise yourself with the Standards before attending your placement. Students are to reflect on ONE chosen Enabling Competency from the NCS in each reflection. Each selected Enabling Competency should be different from the others. Students should reflect on the level of skills, knowledge or professional attributes they acquired/demonstrated during their placement experience and relate this clearly to the chosen Enabling Competency. Students are also encouraged to reflect on their own unique personal experiences (e.g. feelings, personal attributes, individual strengths/weaknesses at this stage of development, etc). 

2. Lifelong Learning (LLL) schema: Students should reflect on their own future learning needs and how they have used feedback to inform their ongoing development, which will enable them to professionally manage their internship and joining the pharmacy profession. They should focus on how their placement uncovered specific elements of their own practice and learning that they consider to be particularly relevant to their future practice, and for becoming a lifelong learner who will remain committed to their continuing professional development. Students should focus on reflecting on ONE or TWO experiences that highlight the evolving nature of the pharmacy industry and how the experience has highlighted specific gaps in their own knowledge or development, focusing on a clear learning plan to bridge the gaps identified from the experience, and consider how they can plan to maintain ongoing lifelong learning throughout their careers.

3. Employability schema: Employability can be defined as a “set of capabilities and personal attributes that empower graduates to perform effectively in the workplace, generate opportunities, and create positive social and economic impact”. This Employability Reflection is an opportunity for students to reflect on their own personal experiences in relation to any aspects of their individual employability at this late stage of their student career path. It is strongly recommended that, before their placement, students familiarise themselves with UQ’s Employability Framework for necessary background information on the meaning, definitions, structure, and relevant considerations in preparation for addressing this assessment item: https://employability.uq.edu.au/about/employability-framework. In this schema, students should focus on personal or professional attributes (rather than clinical skills or knowledge) that can be further developed to aid future employability for their internship and onwards.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools may be used appropriately to support students in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly acknowledge any use of AI in each instance. A failure to acknowledge generative AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submission: This task is uploaded into the ePortfolio.

assessments.assessment_title: Placement Participation, Entrustable Professional Activities (EPAs), EPA Reflection and Individual Learning Plan (ILP)
assessments.task_description:
PLACEMENT PARTICIPATION 

  • Each student must attend and complete their placement for 6 consecutive weeks, 4 days per week and at least 180 hours, in their placement pharmacy, commencing two weeks prior to Week 1 of semester and concluding at the end of Week 4. 
  • Any alterations to the set timeframe must be discussed with and approved by the Course Coordinator prior to the placement. 
  • The start and finish time each day will depend on the agreement between the student and their preceptor. If the placement includes a public holiday relevant to that placement site, the students are expected to attend/not attend the placement site in accordance with usual practice at that site, e.g. if the facility is not open during the public holiday, then the students do not attend and that time is included within the 180 hours. 
  • In the case of illness, students are NOT expected to attend their placement and should inform their preceptor. Absences should be recorded in InPlace within 24 hours of your absence (instructions here). Students away from their placement site for more than TWO days due to illness (i.e. on the third day) should obtain a medical certificate and provide this to the School Office and their preceptor. 
  • Students are advised to contact the Course Coordinator to report any prolonged absence (more than THREE days) from the placement site due to illness. Additionally, irrespective of medical certificates, any cumulative absence over the 6-week period of longer than 3 days must be immediately brought to the attention of the Course Coordinator. 
  • All students are expected to be available to commence classes in other Year 4 courses in Week 5 of semester. 

ENTRUSTABLE PROFESSIONAL ACTIVITIES (EPAs) 

Evidence of entrustment discussions with your preceptor during placement and final entrustment decision at the end of your placement should be completed via the ePortfolio. Students will choose a minimum of THREE (3) EPAs from the suite of provided EPAs that are relevant to the placement site and the students’ learning goals. Students are required to submit a statement of justification for their choice of EPAs with their EPA self-assessment. See the Learn.UQ (Blackboard) site for more information regarding the EPAs.

EPA REFLECTION and INDIVIDUAL LEARNING PLAN (ILP) 

After the placement period is complete, students are required to complete their Reflection and Individual Learning Plan (ILP) for submission via the ePortfolio, based on the feedback they have received from their preceptor or other colleagues at the placement site, the entrustment levels assigned for each EPA undertaken and the action plan discussed during the final feedback session with the preceptor. See Learn.UQ (Blackboard) for more information and marking criteria. 

  • Student Reflection: This should be uploaded as a separate WORD.doc file in the ePortfolio. The maximum word count for the student reflection is 500 words. Students can use the SEAL reflection template if they wish, but can also follow a more generalised structure to reflect on their overall experience in undertaking the chosen EPAs during their placement. It is important for students to focus on their learnings, linking actions to their future development. Students can include references or attachments where necessary/appropriate to support their reflection.
  • Individual Learning Plan (ILP): The ePortfolio provides clear instructions and a template for students to complete their ILP based on their performance in undertaking the EPAs and feedback from their preceptor during their placement. Students should complete each of the relevant boxes in the template prior to submission. 

Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools may be used appropriately to support students in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly acknowledge any use of AI in each instance. A failure to acknowledge generative AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submission: The EPA self-reflections, EPA Reflection and ILP are submitted via the ePortfolio by the due date. The completed EPA self-reflections will be submitted to the student's preceptor via ePortfolio. This will trigger an email to be sent to the Preceptor with a link to complete the student's final EPA entrustment levels and confirm the student's attendance at the placement site via the ePortfolio.

assessments.assessment_title: Optimising Socially Accountable and Holistic Patient Care
assessments.task_description:
Patient-centred care is the fundamental foundation that underpins all aspects of pharmacy practice. Recognising the cultural, social and physical diversity of patients is essential to the delivery of a holistic healthcare solution. Socially accountable care focuses on a health care approach where practices are oriented towards community and social impact, promoting equity and prioritising the social determinants of health such as education, employment and environment, while holistic care considers treating the person as a whole, not just as a disease or symptom, and integrates mental, emotional, spiritual, physical and social needs into the care provided. 

As part of building an increased awareness of the diversity of patients encountered across the Community Pharmacy setting, students are required to submit a report addressing the following: 

  • Identify a particular group of patients serviced by your pharmacy. Some examples may include, but are not limited to, people in the following categories: 
  • Physical or Cognitive Disability 
  • Aged care/Elderly 
  • Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander People 
  • Mental Illness 
  • Culturally/Linguistically Diverse People 
  • Patients on opioid replacement therapy 
  • Low socioeconomic demographic
  • Paediatric population
  • Consider how your pharmacy caters for the socially accountable and holistic care of your selected patient demographic. Consider a broader scope beyond just the usual clinical duties of a pharmacist (i.e. duties such as Webster packs, MedsCheck, and medication counselling would be considered usual clinical duties, so students should explore other aspects of socially accountable and holistic care beyond these services). Some questions to consider may include: 
  • What protocols/procedures/trainings (if any) are in place to assist in the care of these patients (e.g., specially trained staff)? 
  • What physical assistance (if any) does your pharmacy provide (e.g. wheelchair ramp access, wide aisles, etc)? 
  • How does the physical layout of the pharmacy consider patients in your specific patient demographic? 
  • Discuss with your preceptor or other pharmacy staff and consider interviewing some patients/carers for feedback and to gather anecdotal evidence of their personal experiences. Students are encouraged to think creatively about how to gather relevant information. 
  • Perform a literature review of the current evidence for best practice in the care of patients in your chosen demographic. Consider recommendations from relevant governing or professional bodies, Royal Commission recommendations, and relevant publications from contemporary research. 
  • Identify any gaps between current and best practice and create a plan specific to your placement pharmacy to address gaps/implement any new recommendations to optimise socially accountable and holistic patient care in your chosen patient population. 

The report should be a maximum of 2000 words, and students should follow the report structure outlined on Learn.UQ (Blackboard). The marking criteria/rubric for this assessment task is available on Learn.UQ (Blackboard).

Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools may be used appropriately to support students in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly acknowledge any use of AI in each instance. A failure to acknowledge generative AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

learning_activities.topic:
Online Module - Course Introduction

The Course Introduction module can be completed by students anytime prior to the commencement of their placement. It can be accessed online via the course Learn.UQ site. It relays important information about the placement, assessment, and the Socially Accountable and Holistic Care Assignment. There is a short quiz made up of 10 multiple choice questions following the completion of the module. You must achieve at least 80% on the quiz, and you are allowed multiple attempts. The quiz does not count towards your course grade.

The module will cover the following:

  • Introduction to PHRM4072
  • Placements
  • Assessment overview and expectations
  • Entrustable Professional Activities (EPAs) and Individual Learning Plan
  • Socially Accountable and Holistic Care

You must complete this module and successfully pass the quiz before you can access the Assessments folder. This will allow you to access the assessment submission links as well as information regarding course assessments.

learning_activities.topic:
Online Introductory Lecture

Online Introductory Lecture: The Course Coordinator will provide a course introduction, an overview of the placement and work-integrated learning, and give guidance on how to perform productively and successfully in this course. The lecture will be an opportunity for students to ask questions and clarify any concerns prior to commencing their placements. The online lecture will take place approximately two weeks before placements commence (4 weeks before Week 1 of semester).

This Lecture will be held online on Tuesday, 30th June 2026 at 1pm - See Learn.UQ (Blackboard) for the Zoom link.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 3 To Week 8(13 Jul - 23 Aug)
learning_activities.topic:
Community Pharmacy Practice Placement

  • A supervised experiential placement undertaken in an Australian community pharmacy
  • Complete at least 180 hours over 6 weeks, 4 days per week, commencing 2 weeks prior to Week 1 of the semester. 
  • During the experiential placement, students choose to participate in a minimum of THREE EPAs from a suite of EPAs provided, based on the context of their placement.  
  • These may be done as a one-off, or there may be multiple opportunities to conduct these activities under observation by the preceptor.

These may be done as a one-off, or there may be multiple opportunities to conduct these activities under observation by the preceptor.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 3 To Week 8(13 Jul - 23 Aug)
learning_activities.topic:
Ongoing Preceptor & Peer Feedback, Discussion, Evaluation

Students are encouraged to actively seek out appropriate opportunities to discuss their placement progress with their Preceptor and ask for feedback on an ongoing basis. Students are also encouraged to seek peer feedback on their reflections to inform the development of their professional skills, including reflective practice.
